summary: ʿAdl is the doctrine of divine justice, the principle that God acts only justly and does no wrong. It is one of the five fundamental principles (al-usul al-khamsa) of the Mu'tazila and a defining commitment of their theology.
description: ʿAdl affirms that God is just and that injustice cannot be attributed to Him. For the Mu'tazila it is the second of their five principles (al-usul al-khamsa), after divine oneness (tawhid). From this principle the Mu'tazila drew far-reaching conclusions: because a just God cannot fairly punish acts He Himself compels, human beings must possess genuine causal power over their own actions, and so the Mu'tazila affirmed human free will. On this view attributing all human acts to God would render divine reward and punishment unjust. The principle also shaped Mu'tazili treatments of good and evil, obligation, and what God does for creatures. The Ash'ari school contested this framework. Where the Mu'tazila appealed to divine justice to include human beings within the circle of real agents, al-Ash'ari and his followers appealed to divine omnipotence to reserve real causal efficacy to God alone, developing the doctrine of kasb (acquisition), whereby God creates the act and its accompanying created power in the human, who thereby "acquires" it and bears responsibility. The two schools thus prioritized differently: the Mu'tazila foregrounded justice, the Ash'ariyya omnipotence. This debate is closely bound up with qadar (divine determination) and with the reconciliation of human responsibility and divine decree. The doctrine is also central to Twelver Shi'i theology, which counts ʿadl among its roots of religion.
editorial_notes: This entry frames ʿadl primarily as the Mu'tazili principle the prompt identifies, while noting that divine justice is also a foundational commitment in Twelver Shi'i theology. The Mu'tazila / Ash'ari contrast is presented as two attributed positions and is not adjudicated, per the neutrality policy; the associated Mu'tazili claim that God is obligated to do what is optimal (al-aslah) for creatures is a further, internally debated sub-doctrine deferred to a dedicated entry. Sourcing for the Mu'tazila–Ash'ari contrast, kasb, and the justice-vs-omnipotence framing rests on the Stanford Encyclopedia of Philosophy entry on causation in Arabic and Islamic thought (high confidence); the enumeration of ʿadl within al-usul al-khamsa rests on the additional reference below. To triangulate these with the tradition's own primary literature (neutrality principle #8), ʿAbd al-Jabbār's Sharḥ al-Uṣūl al-Khamsa, the classic Muʿtazilī exposition in which ʿadl is the second principle, is appended at medium confidence, as its relevant passages were not read line-by-line here.

relations.note: ʿAdl is the second of the Mu'tazila's five principles and a defining commitment.
One of the five fundamental Mu'tazili principles.
Paired with tawhid as the two most emphasized Mu'tazili principles.
Divine justice grounds the Mu'tazili affirmation of free will against unqualified predestination.
The Ash'ari doctrine of acquisition, framed around divine omnipotence rather than justice.
Divine justice underwrites recompense in the hereafter.

historical_period.note: Central to Mu'tazili theology from its early development in Basra and Baghdad (8th century) through the classical debates with the Ash'ariyya (10th–11th centuries).

sources.citation: Richardson, Kara. "Causation in Arabic and Islamic Thought." In The Stanford Encyclopedia of Philosophy, ed. Edward N. Zalta. Metaphysics Research Lab, Stanford University, 2025.
sources.license: Open access (Creative Commons; SEP is free to read online). Cited for reference.
CC BY-SA (encyclopedic wiki). Cited for the enumeration of the five principles.

sources.citation: ʿAbd al-Jabbār al-Hamadhānī, al-Qāḍī. Sharḥ al-Uṣūl al-Khamsa [Commentary on the Five Principles]. Muʿtazilī kalām work (late 10th / early 11th c. CE). The classic exposition of the five principles, in which divine justice (ʿadl), that God does no wrong and cannot be the author of human injustice, so that humans must possess real power over their acts, is elaborated as the second principle.
